industry.name: Space Industry
industry.description: Spacecraft design, propulsion, launch operations, mission control, and the commercial space economy. 90 roles across 6 clusters.

roles.title: Spacecraft Design Engineer 1
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or a closely related STEM field is strictly required for this entry-level position. While a four-year degree is the standard baseline, candidates with foundational coursework in orbital mechanics, materials science, or propulsion systems are highly preferred. Advanced study such as a Master’s degree in Astronautical Engineering can be beneficial but is not mandatory for initial entry. Professional certifications like the EIT (Engineer-in-Training) designation are viewed as strong indicators of technical proficiency and commitment to the field.

skills.name: Computer Aided Design Proficiency
skills.description: You will utilize industry-standard software like SolidWorks or NX to develop complex 3D models and 2D technical drawings. This skill is essential for translating conceptual designs into manufacturable space-grade components.

skills.name: Spacecraft Subsystems Integration Knowledge
skills.description: Understanding how individual subsystems like power, propulsion, and avionics interact is critical. You must ensure your mechanical designs accommodate the physical and electrical interfaces required for successful satellite operation.

skills.name: Structural Analysis And Simulation
skills.description: You will perform finite element analysis to predict how components respond to launch loads and orbital stressors. This ensures structural integrity throughout the spacecraft's entire mission lifecycle.

skills.name: Technical Requirements Analysis
skills.description: You must be capable of parsing complex mission requirements to define design constraints. This involves documenting how your hardware meets specific environmental, mass, and performance targets set by systems engineering.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: Applying GD&T standards ensures that parts are manufactured with the precision required for high-stakes aerospace assembly. This skill minimizes production errors and guarantees perfect fitment during final spacecraft integration.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: Clear communication through design reviews and technical reports is vital for team transparency. You must document your design choices and validation results to maintain compliance with rigorous flight certification standards.

roles.description: As a Spacecraft Design Engineer 1, you serve as a foundational contributor within the engineering team, transforming high-level mission objectives into tangible hardware solutions. Your day-to-day involves creating detailed CAD models, performing initial structural analyses, and documenting subsystem specifications under the direct mentorship of senior staff. You are responsible for ensuring that individual component designs adhere to rigorous aerospace standards while meeting strict mass, power, and thermal constraints. You fit into the value chain by bridging the gap between theoretical mission architecture and flight-ready hardware assembly. Individuals who thrive in this role possess a meticulous attention to detail, a collaborative mindset, and an unwavering commitment to safety and precision in the unforgiving environment of space.

roles.title: Avionics Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Electrical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, or Computer Engineering is the standard requirement for this position. While a four-year degree is essential, many employers look for candidates who have completed coursework in digital signal processing, control systems, or electromagnetic compatibility. Pursuing a Master’s degree in a specialized field like Space Systems Engineering can provide a competitive edge, though it is not strictly mandatory. Professional certifications in specific CAD software or project management methodologies are often viewed as valuable assets for mid-level engineers looking to demonstrate advanced technical proficiency.

skills.name: Radiation Hardened Circuit Design
skills.description: You must design electronic circuits capable of maintaining operational integrity despite exposure to ionizing radiation. This involves selecting specialized components and implementing shielding techniques to prevent single-event upsets or permanent hardware degradation.

skills.name: Embedded Systems Firmware Development
skills.description: You will develop low-level firmware to interface between hardware components and flight software. Proficiency in C or C++ is critical for managing real-time data processing and hardware-level communication protocols within the spacecraft.

skills.name: High Speed PCB Design
skills.description: This skill involves creating complex, multi-layer printed circuit board layouts that manage signal integrity and thermal dissipation. You must balance high-density component placement with the strict constraints required for space-grade flight hardware.

skills.name: Space Environment Testing Protocols
skills.description: You are responsible for executing environmental stress screening, including thermal vacuum and vibration testing. These protocols ensure that your electronic designs can withstand the extreme mechanical and thermal loads of launch and orbital insertion.

skills.name: Flight Hardware Integration Testing
skills.description: You will lead the verification of electronic subsystems through rigorous hardware-in-the-loop testing. This ensures that your flight computers and sensors communicate effectively with the spacecraft’s primary command and data handling systems.

skills.name: Electronic CAD Tool Proficiency
skills.description: Mastery of industry-standard EDA tools like Altium Designer or Cadence Allegro is essential for schematic capture and PCB layout. You use these tools to maintain design documentation and prepare files for manufacturing.

roles.description: As a mid-level Avionics Engineer, you serve as the technical backbone for spacecraft mission success, bridging the gap between theoretical system requirements and flight-ready hardware. Your day-to-day involves designing, prototyping, and rigorously testing critical electronic systems, including flight computers, power distribution units, and complex sensor suites. You are responsible for ensuring these components survive the harsh vacuum and radiation environments of space while maintaining mission-critical reliability. Working within a multidisciplinary spacecraft design team, you collaborate closely with systems and software engineers to integrate your hardware into the broader vehicle architecture. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous attention to detail, a passion for solving complex electrical challenges, and the resilience to iterate through rigorous testing cycles.

roles.title: Spacecraft Systems Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Electrical Engineering is typically the minimum requirement for this role. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundational knowledge, many professionals further distinguish themselves by pursuing a Master’s degree in Systems Engineering or Space Systems. Professional certifications such as INCOSE Systems Engineering Professional (SEP) are highly regarded and often preferred for mid-level advancement. While the path is generally technical, some flexibility exists for candidates with physics or computer science backgrounds if they possess significant relevant experience in complex system architectures.

skills.name: Spacecraft Systems Integration
skills.description: This involves the physical and logical assembly of disparate subsystems into a cohesive spacecraft. You ensure that all electrical, mechanical, and software interfaces function correctly to meet mission-critical performance requirements.

skills.name: Technical Requirements Management
skills.description: You define, track, and verify technical requirements throughout the vehicle lifecycle. This process ensures that every engineering decision remains traceable to high-level mission goals and safety standards.

skills.name: Model Based Systems Engineering
skills.description: Utilizing MBSE methodologies, you create digital models to manage complex system architectures. This approach improves documentation clarity and allows for more efficient simulation of system performance under various mission conditions.

skills.name: Subsystem Trade Study Analysis
skills.description: You perform quantitative evaluations to compare design alternatives against performance, cost, and schedule constraints. These studies are essential for making data-driven decisions that optimize the overall spacecraft architecture.

skills.name: Space Mission Verification Planning
skills.description: You develop comprehensive test and verification plans to confirm that the spacecraft meets all design specifications. This ensures the vehicle can survive the harsh launch environment and operate reliably in orbit.

skills.name: Cross Functional Engineering Collaboration
skills.description: You facilitate communication between diverse engineering disciplines to resolve technical bottlenecks. Your ability to translate complex system-level impacts to subsystem leads is vital for maintaining project momentum and design integrity.

roles.description: As a mid-level Spacecraft Systems Engineer, you serve as the technical orchestrator, bridging the gap between specialized subsystem teams to ensure the entire vehicle functions as a unified, reliable mission asset. Your day-to-day involves managing complex technical requirements, conducting rigorous trade studies to optimize mass and power budgets, and verifying that design iterations align with mission objectives. You act as the central point of contact for cross-functional engineering teams, resolving interface conflicts and ensuring seamless integration from initial design through to final flight readiness. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, exceptional communication skills to navigate competing engineering priorities, and a deep-seated curiosity for solving the multifaceted challenges inherent in modern space exploration.

roles.title: Thermal Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Physics is the standard foundation for this career path. While a bachelor's is the minimum requirement, many professionals choose to pursue a Master’s degree in Thermal-Fluids or Space Systems Engineering to gain specialized knowledge in advanced heat transfer. Obtaining a Professional Engineer (PE) license can be beneficial, though it is not strictly required in the private space sector. Demonstrating proficiency through internships or academic projects involving thermal vacuum testing is highly valued by hiring managers.

skills.name: Advanced Thermal Desktop Modeling
skills.description: You will utilize Thermal Desktop to construct complex geometric models of spacecraft. This skill is essential for performing accurate nodal analysis and predicting temperature profiles under various orbital heating conditions.

skills.name: Heat Transfer Analysis Principles
skills.description: Mastery of conduction, convection, and radiation is vital for designing effective thermal control systems. You must apply these fundamental physics principles to solve complex heat rejection challenges in a vacuum.

skills.name: Spacecraft Thermal Control Systems
skills.description: You are responsible for the selection and integration of passive and active hardware, such as heaters, louvers, and phase change materials. This ensures mission success by maintaining stable internal environments.

skills.name: Thermal Vacuum Testing Procedures
skills.description: You will lead or support testing campaigns in vacuum chambers to validate your analytical models against real-world data. This process is critical for flight qualification and hardware risk mitigation.

skills.name: Computational Fluid Dynamics Simulation
skills.description: Leveraging CFD tools allows you to analyze internal airflow and fluid loops within the spacecraft. This capability helps optimize cooling system performance for high-power electronics and sensitive payload instruments.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: Clear communication of thermal margins and design risks is essential for interdisciplinary collaboration. You must document your findings to support formal design reviews and mission assurance requirements.

roles.description: As a Thermal Engineer, you serve as the critical guardian of spacecraft integrity, ensuring that sensitive electronics and hardware survive the unforgiving thermal environment of space. Your day-to-day involves developing sophisticated thermal models to simulate orbital heating and cooling cycles, collaborating closely with structural and electrical teams to integrate thermal protection systems like multi-layer insulation, heat pipes, and radiators. You are responsible for verifying that every component remains within strict operational temperature limits throughout all mission phases. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a deep passion for thermodynamics, and the ability to balance complex mission constraints with practical hardware implementation. Your work directly dictates the operational lifespan and reliability of high-value space assets.

roles.title: GNC Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A graduate degree is required for this role, typically a Master of Science in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Robotics. Your coursework should prioritize advanced control theory, astrodynamics, and state estimation, providing the foundational knowledge necessary for complex trajectory design. While a Master’s degree is the standard, candidates with a PhD in a specialized field like optimal control or guidance systems often hold a significant advantage. Pursuing an advanced degree demonstrates the technical depth required to navigate the sophisticated analytical challenges inherent in modern spacecraft mission design.

skills.name: Guidance Navigation And Control Systems
skills.description: You will design and implement the core algorithms that determine spacecraft position, velocity, and orientation. This includes developing robust control laws to maintain stability during complex orbital maneuvers.

skills.name: Orbital Mechanics And Trajectory Design
skills.description: You must apply fundamental principles of astrodynamics to calculate mission-critical flight paths. This involves modeling multi-body perturbations and optimizing fuel consumption for long-duration interplanetary or Earth-orbiting missions.

skills.name: Control Theory And System Modeling
skills.description: Utilizing classical and modern control techniques, you will analyze system stability and performance. This involves creating mathematical models to predict how spacecraft hardware will respond to specific control inputs.

skills.name: MATLAB And Simulink Simulation
skills.description: You will leverage these tools to build high-fidelity simulations for algorithm verification. This environment is essential for testing control logic against various edge cases before deployment to flight hardware.

skills.name: Sensor Fusion And Estimation Theory
skills.description: You will integrate data from diverse sensors like star trackers, gyroscopes, and accelerometers using Kalman filtering. This ensures the spacecraft maintains an accurate state estimate in real-time.

skills.name: Flight Software Integration Principles
skills.description: You must understand how control algorithms translate into embedded code for flight processors. This includes collaborating with software engineers to ensure timing constraints and memory requirements are met.

roles.description: As a mid-level GNC Engineer, you serve as the architect of spacecraft autonomy, translating mission objectives into precise flight dynamics. Your daily responsibilities involve designing, simulating, and validating complex algorithms that govern attitude determination, trajectory optimization, and station-keeping maneuvers. You operate at the critical intersection of systems engineering and software development, ensuring that hardware actuators and sensors function in perfect harmony under extreme environmental constraints. You will collaborate closely with mission analysts and flight software teams to integrate control laws into the vehicle's onboard flight computer. The ideal candidate thrives on high-stakes problem-solving, possesses a rigorous attention to detail, and enjoys the intellectual challenge of refining mathematical models to guarantee mission success in the unforgiving vacuum of space.

roles.title: Senior Spacecraft Systems Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A graduate degree is strictly required for this senior-level position, typically in Aerospace Engineering, Systems Engineering, or a closely related field such as Mechanical or Electrical Engineering. Candidates are expected to hold a Master of Science, though a Ph.D. is highly advantageous for roles involving advanced mission architecture or research-heavy projects. Professional certifications such as INCOSE Systems Engineering Professional (SEP) or specialized project management credentials are often preferred to validate your expertise in managing large-scale technical programs.

skills.name: Full Lifecycle Systems Architecture
skills.description: You lead the end-to-end design process, ensuring seamless integration between hardware, software, and ground segments. This skill involves maintaining system-level technical integrity from the initial mission concept through to launch and operational deployment.

skills.name: Technical Risk Management Strategy
skills.description: You identify, quantify, and mitigate complex technical risks that could jeopardize mission success. This involves rigorous failure mode analysis and the implementation of robust contingency plans to ensure reliable performance under extreme conditions.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Engineering Leadership
skills.description: You act as the primary technical interface between specialized engineering teams, fostering collaboration to resolve design conflicts. Your leadership ensures that all subsystems align with the overarching mission requirements and performance constraints.

skills.name: Mission Assurance And Verification
skills.description: You oversee comprehensive verification and validation protocols to confirm that all spacecraft components meet stringent flight readiness standards. This requires meticulous attention to detail during environmental testing and final orbital qualification.

skills.name: Requirements Engineering And Management
skills.description: You translate high-level stakeholder objectives into actionable, measurable technical specifications. This involves managing requirement traceability throughout the development process to prevent scope creep and ensure compliance with mission-critical performance benchmarks.

skills.name: Spacecraft Subsystem Trade Studies
skills.description: You perform complex trade-off analyses to optimize spacecraft mass, power, and thermal budgets. By evaluating competing design solutions, you ensure the most efficient configuration for mission success within rigid budgetary and physical constraints.

roles.description: As a Senior Spacecraft Systems Engineer, you serve as the technical authority overseeing the complete architecture and mission lifecycle of complex space systems. Your day-to-day involves integrating multifaceted subsystems, managing mission-critical technical risks, and bridging the gap between high-level customer requirements and detailed engineering execution. You are responsible for ensuring that the integrated spacecraft design meets rigorous performance and reliability standards from initial concept through final in-orbit operations. You thrive in this role if you possess a systems-thinking mindset, an ability to navigate ambiguity, and the leadership capacity to mentor multidisciplinary teams. By orchestrating complex trade studies and verification processes, you ensure mission success in the challenging and unforgiving environment of space, directly impacting the strategic objectives of your organization.

roles.title: Chief Engineer (Spacecraft)
roles.degree_detail: A graduate degree is typically required for this role, with a Master of Science or PhD in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Systems Engineering serving as the standard academic foundation. Candidates often benefit from specialized coursework in orbital mechanics, spacecraft dynamics, or space systems architecture to manage the technical breadth required. While formal education is essential, professional certifications such as Professional Engineer (PE) licensure or Project Management Professional (PMP) status can further enhance your credibility in managing large-scale aerospace programs.

skills.name: Mission Architecture And Systems Integration
skills.description: You oversee the end-to-end design of the spacecraft, ensuring all subsystems function harmoniously. This requires a deep understanding of how individual engineering decisions impact the overall mission success and orbital performance.

skills.name: Technical Authority And Design Governance
skills.description: You hold final sign-off on all engineering changes and technical specifications. You must rigorously manage design reviews and verification processes to maintain flight safety and mission integrity throughout the development lifecycle.

skills.name: Multidisciplinary Engineering Leadership
skills.description: You lead diverse teams of engineers across propulsion, GNC, structures, and software departments. You must translate high-level mission goals into actionable technical requirements while fostering a culture of collaborative problem-solving.

skills.name: Risk Management And Trade Study Analysis
skills.description: You evaluate complex engineering trade-offs to mitigate technical risks while balancing mass, power, and budget constraints. Your ability to quantify and manage uncertainty is vital for ensuring spacecraft reliability.

skills.name: Stakeholder Communication And Technical Advocacy
skills.description: You serve as the primary liaison between engineering teams and non-technical executive stakeholders. You must clearly articulate complex technical challenges, mission status, and strategic recommendations to ensure organizational alignment.

skills.name: Spacecraft Verification And Validation
skills.description: You define the protocols for testing and validating hardware and software prior to launch. This involves overseeing environmental testing and simulation campaigns to guarantee the craft survives launch and orbital conditions.

roles.description: As a Chief Engineer for spacecraft, you serve as the ultimate technical authority, orchestrating the entire lifecycle of complex orbital or deep-space missions. You own the mission architecture, balancing rigorous engineering trade-offs against stringent mission requirements and safety constraints. Day-to-day, you lead multidisciplinary teams through critical design reviews, verifying that every subsystem—from propulsion to avionics—integrates seamlessly into a reliable flight vehicle. You act as the primary technical bridge between executive leadership and engineering staff, ensuring that mission success remains the north star amidst evolving technical challenges. You thrive in this role if you possess an unwavering commitment to technical excellence, the ability to synthesize disparate data into coherent strategies, and the resilience to make high-stakes decisions under extreme pressure.

roles.title: Propulsion Engineer 1
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or a closely related physical science discipline is required to establish the necessary technical foundation for this role. While a four-year degree is the standard entry point, coursework focusing on fluid dynamics, heat transfer, and combustion science is highly advantageous for applicants. Additional certifications in specialized CAD software or hands-on experience through university rocketry teams can significantly strengthen your candidacy. While this role strictly requires a Bachelor's level education, candidates pursuing a Master's degree with a focus on propulsion systems may find their advanced theoretical knowledge particularly beneficial in this specialized field.

skills.name: Propulsion Systems Fundamentals
skills.description: You must understand the core principles of chemical propulsion, including rocket engine cycles and nozzle theory. This knowledge allows you to support the design and performance evaluation of flight hardware.

skills.name: Fluid Systems And Plumbing
skills.description: You will manage the design and integration of propellant feed systems, valves, and regulators. This skill ensures the safe and efficient delivery of cryogenic or storable propellants to the engine.

skills.name: Computer Aided Design Proficiency
skills.description: Utilizing industry-standard CAD software, you will create detailed 3D models and technical drawings for engine components. This is essential for turning conceptual propulsion designs into manufacturable hardware parts.

skills.name: Test Support And Instrumentation
skills.description: You will assist in the setup and execution of ground testing by monitoring data acquisition systems and sensors. This ensures that engine performance metrics are captured accurately during hot-fire events.

skills.name: Thermal And Fluid Analysis
skills.description: Applying computational fluid dynamics and thermal modeling tools helps you predict how systems behave under extreme flight environments. These simulations are critical for verifying structural integrity before hardware is built.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You must clearly document test results, design changes, and engineering analyses for team review. Strong technical writing ensures that institutional knowledge is preserved and that safety protocols are strictly followed.

roles.description: As a Propulsion Engineer 1, you serve as a vital contributor to the development and validation of high-performance rocket engine components and complex fluid systems. Your day-to-day responsibilities include assisting senior engineers with CAD modeling, performing thermal-fluid analysis, and actively supporting ground-based hot-fire testing operations. You play a key role in the mission-critical value chain by ensuring that propulsion hardware meets stringent flight-readiness standards through rigorous data collection and troubleshooting. This position is ideal for a detail-oriented individual who thrives in fast-paced, collaborative environments and possesses a deep-seated passion for aerospace thermodynamics. You will succeed by maintaining focus on technical precision, clear communication, and a proactive approach to solving complex engineering challenges within the evolving landscape of space exploration.

roles.title: Propulsion Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Aerospace, Mechanical, or Chemical Engineering is the standard foundational requirement for this position. While a four-year degree is essential, many professionals in the field pursue a Master’s degree to gain specialized knowledge in advanced propulsion or combustion dynamics. Professional certifications in project management or specific CAD software suites can further distinguish your profile, though they are generally considered supplementary to your core academic and practical engineering training.

skills.name: Rocket Engine System Design
skills.description: You will conceptualize and refine complex propulsion architectures, balancing performance metrics with manufacturing constraints. This skill involves integrating subsystems like injectors, turbopumps, and nozzles to ensure optimal thrust and stability.

skills.name: Computational Fluid Dynamics Analysis
skills.description: Utilizing advanced simulation tools, you will model internal flow fields and thermal distributions. This is critical for predicting engine behavior and identifying potential failure modes before physical hardware is ever manufactured.

skills.name: Thermodynamics And Combustion Modeling
skills.description: You will apply fundamental heat transfer and chemical kinetics principles to optimize combustion efficiency. This ensures that propellant mixtures react predictably under extreme pressures and temperatures within the combustion chamber.

skills.name: Propulsion Hardware Testing Protocols
skills.description: You are responsible for designing and executing ground test campaigns to validate engine performance. This involves interpreting sensor data, managing test-stand instrumentation, and ensuring all safety protocols are strictly followed.

skills.name: Fluid Systems Integration Engineering
skills.description: This involves managing the complex plumbing, valves, and feed systems that deliver propellants to the engine. You must ensure seamless compatibility between fluid dynamics requirements and the overall vehicle architecture.

skills.name: Structural Analysis For Propulsion
skills.description: You will evaluate how propulsion components react to high-stress flight environments and vibrational loads. This ensures structural integrity is maintained throughout the entire mission profile, from ignition through orbital insertion.

roles.description: As a mid-level Propulsion Engineer, you serve as a critical technical bridge, translating complex mission requirements into high-performance flight hardware. Your day-to-day involves conducting rigorous analytical modeling, overseeing experimental test campaigns, and iterating on engine architectures to optimize thrust-to-weight ratios and propellant efficiency. You are responsible for the end-to-end lifecycle of propulsion components, ensuring seamless integration between fluid subsystems and structural interfaces. Thriving in this role requires a meticulous attention to detail, a deep-seated passion for aerospace physics, and the collaborative spirit necessary to navigate fast-paced, multidisciplinary environments. You must be comfortable balancing theoretical CFD simulations with the gritty, hands-on realities of hardware testing, ultimately ensuring that every engine you develop delivers reliable, precise orbital maneuvering capabilities.

roles.title: Propulsion Test Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Aerospace, Mechanical, or Electrical Engineering is the standard entry point for this role. While a four-year degree is strictly required, candidates often supplement their technical foundation with coursework in thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, or propulsion systems. Pursuing a Master’s degree in a specialized propulsion-related field is highly preferred for mid-level roles, though not strictly mandatory. Professional certifications in project management or specific data acquisition software can also bolster your candidacy by demonstrating a commitment to industry-standard engineering practices.

skills.name: Engine Hot Fire Testing
skills.description: You will manage the end-to-end execution of ground-based engine firing sequences. This involves coordinating propellant loading, ignition timing, and emergency shutdown procedures to ensure successful test objectives.

skills.name: Test Stand Systems Operation
skills.description: You are responsible for the safe operation of high-pressure test facilities. This includes managing fluid control systems, cryogenic storage, and structural support hardware during high-energy propulsion testing.

skills.name: Data Acquisition And Instrumentation
skills.description: You will deploy and calibrate sensors such as thermocouples and pressure transducers to capture high-fidelity performance metrics. You must effectively translate raw signal data into actionable engineering insights.

skills.name: Propulsion System Performance Analysis
skills.description: You will interpret complex datasets to evaluate engine health, thrust efficiency, and combustion stability. This analysis is critical for identifying potential failure modes and verifying design specifications.

skills.name: Fluid And Gas Systems Design
skills.description: You must understand the piping, valves, and control architecture required to deliver propellants to the engine. Proficiency in fluid mechanics ensures safe and reliable flow during critical test phases.

skills.name: Safety And Hazard Analysis
skills.description: You will perform rigorous risk assessments for all test operations to mitigate hazards associated with high-pressure and high-temperature environments. Maintaining strict adherence to aerospace safety protocols is essential.

roles.description: As a Propulsion Test Engineer, you bridge the gap between theoretical engine design and flight-ready hardware by orchestrating critical hot-fire test campaigns. Your daily responsibilities involve configuring complex fluid and electrical systems, managing high-pressure test stand operations, and meticulously analyzing performance data to validate combustion stability and structural integrity. You serve as a vital link in the propulsion development cycle, collaborating closely with design engineers to iterate on hardware based on empirical performance metrics. You thrive in this role if you possess a rigorous, safety-conscious mindset, an ability to troubleshoot hardware anomalies under pressure, and a deep-seated passion for pushing the boundaries of rocket engine performance through iterative, data-driven experimentation in a fast-paced aerospace environment.

roles.title: Power Systems Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Electrical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, or a closely related physical science is the standard requirement for this position. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundation in circuit analysis and power electronics, many candidates choose to pursue a Master’s degree to gain specialized knowledge in spacecraft power systems. Professional certifications in systems engineering or project management can also distinguish your application. While the degree requirement is firm, demonstrated hands-on experience with satellite hardware or high-reliability power electronics often holds equal weight during the recruitment process.

skills.name: Spacecraft EPS Design
skills.description: You will design and analyze Electrical Power Systems, ensuring that energy generation, storage, and distribution architectures meet mission requirements. This involves rigorous modeling of power budgets throughout all mission phases.

skills.name: Solar Array Performance Analysis
skills.description: You must evaluate solar cell efficiency and degradation over time, accounting for radiation environments and orbital parameters. This skill is vital for predicting the long-term energy output of a spacecraft.

skills.name: Battery Management Systems
skills.description: You will manage the integration and health monitoring of lithium-ion or other battery chemistries for space applications. This includes defining charging profiles and depth-of-discharge limits to maximize cycle life.

skills.name: Power Distribution Unit Engineering
skills.description: This involves designing the circuitry that routes power from the bus to various spacecraft subsystems. You will ensure robust protection, switching, and regulation to maintain mission-critical stability.

skills.name: Electrical System Modeling Simulation
skills.description: You will utilize industry-standard software tools to simulate power transients and thermal interactions. These simulations are essential for validating system performance before flight hardware is ever manufactured.

skills.name: Space Environment Effects Analysis
skills.description: You must account for the harsh conditions of space, including atomic oxygen, vacuum, and ionizing radiation. Understanding these effects ensures that power components remain reliable throughout the entire mission lifespan.

roles.description: As a Power Systems Engineer, you are the architect of a spacecraft’s lifeblood, ensuring mission success through reliable energy generation and management. In this mid-level role, you oversee the end-to-end lifecycle of electrical power systems, from sizing solar arrays and battery capacity to designing complex power distribution units. You collaborate closely with thermal and systems engineering teams to ensure your hardware integrates seamlessly within strict mass and volume constraints. You thrive in this position if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a deep understanding of orbital electrical loads, and the ability to troubleshoot complex anomalies under pressure. Your work directly dictates the operational longevity of satellites, making you a critical link in the aerospace value chain.

roles.title: Senior Propulsion Engineer
roles.degree_detail: This role requires a Graduate Degree, typically a Master of Science in Aerospace or Mechanical Engineering, to ensure a robust foundation in advanced thermodynamics and propulsion physics. Candidates often pursue specialized coursework in liquid rocket propulsion, combustion, or cryogenic systems to gain a competitive edge. While a PhD is not strictly mandatory, it is highly valued for research-heavy roles or positions focused on next-generation engine cycles. Professional certifications, such as those related to systems engineering or project management, can further demonstrate your ability to handle complex, multidisciplinary aerospace projects.

skills.name: Propulsion System Architecture Design
skills.description: You will conceptualize and define the high-level architecture for liquid or solid propulsion systems. This involves balancing performance requirements with mass, volume, and cost constraints to meet mission-specific objectives.

skills.name: Rocket Engine Development Lifecycle
skills.description: You manage the full development cycle, moving hardware from preliminary design through critical design reviews and into final qualification. This requires deep familiarity with rapid prototyping and iterative testing cycles.

skills.name: Technical Leadership And Mentorship
skills.description: You provide senior-level guidance to junior engineers, fostering technical growth and ensuring best practices are followed. You are responsible for fostering a culture of safety and rigorous design integrity.

skills.name: Advanced Fluid Dynamics Analysis
skills.description: You utilize computational fluid dynamics and analytical modeling to predict performance and thermal behavior. Mastering these tools is essential for optimizing combustion stability and preventing hardware failure during operation.

skills.name: Qualification And Testing Oversight
skills.description: You lead the test planning and verification process, ensuring all propulsion hardware adheres to flight-readiness standards. This includes interpreting test data to resolve anomalies and validating performance against mission requirements.

skills.name: Systems Engineering Integration
skills.description: You coordinate with avionics, structures, and thermal teams to ensure seamless propulsion integration. This skill focuses on managing cross-disciplinary interfaces to avoid system-level conflicts during the vehicle development process.

roles.description: As a Senior Propulsion Engineer, you serve as the technical authority for complex rocket engine development programs, guiding systems from initial architecture through rigorous qualification testing. You are responsible for overseeing critical design reviews, mitigating integration risks, and ensuring that propulsion hardware meets stringent performance and safety requirements. Within the value chain, you act as a bridge between high-level mission objectives and detailed component engineering, mentoring junior staff to foster technical excellence across the team. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a deep understanding of fluid dynamics and combustion stability, and the ability to maintain composure while managing high-stakes technical trade-offs in a fast-paced aerospace environment.

roles.title: Propulsion Lead / Manager
roles.degree_detail: A graduate degree is strictly required for this leadership role, typically a Master of Science or PhD in Aerospace Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Applied Physics. Advanced coursework in rocket propulsion systems, combustion instability, or cryogenic fluid dynamics is highly valued. While a formal degree is essential, professional certifications such as Project Management Professional or specialized training in systems engineering methodologies can further distinguish your candidacy for senior management positions.

skills.name: Propulsion Program Lifecycle Ownership
skills.description: You oversee the entire propulsion development cycle from initial conceptual design and trade studies through to final flight qualification and hardware delivery. This requires deep expertise in managing complex engineering milestones.

skills.name: Strategic Cross-Functional Team Leadership
skills.description: You manage diverse teams of engineers, providing technical mentorship and aligning individual contributions with overarching mission objectives. Effective communication across departments is vital for resolving integrated system conflicts.

skills.name: Technical Risk And Budget Management
skills.description: You are responsible for balancing technical performance requirements against strict financial constraints and schedule limitations. This involves proactive risk mitigation and resource allocation to ensure mission success within budget.

skills.name: Advanced Propulsion Systems Architecture
skills.description: You define the foundational design parameters for liquid, solid, or electric propulsion systems. Your deep understanding of thermodynamics and fluid mechanics ensures the vehicle meets all performance and orbital requirements.

skills.name: Flight Qualification And Compliance
skills.description: You navigate complex regulatory frameworks and safety standards to certify propulsion hardware for flight. This requires rigorous documentation and the ability to justify technical decisions to internal and external stakeholders.

skills.name: Systems Integration And Interface Control
skills.description: You ensure seamless integration of propulsion subsystems with the broader vehicle architecture. Managing interface control documents is essential to prevent conflicts between propulsion, avionics, and structural components.

roles.description: As a Propulsion Lead, you own the end-to-end technical strategy and operational execution for complex rocket engine systems and in-space propulsion architectures. You orchestrate cross-functional teams of engineers, analysts, and manufacturing specialists to ensure mission-critical hardware meets stringent performance and safety standards.
